California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

November 8, 1988


Honorable Robert Presley
Senator, Thirty-sixth District
3600 Lime Street, Suite 111
Riverside, CA 92501

Re: Your Request for Assistance
Our File No. I-88-426

Dear Senator Presley:

We have received your letter requesting an opinion
concerning the rights of political candidates to distribute
campaign material in mobilehome parks. We are unable to assist
you in this matter because the subject is outside of the
Commission's jurisdiction.

The Commission is charged with the administration and
enforcement of the Political Reform Act. (Government Code
Sections 81000-91015.) The Political Reform Act requires
candidates to report campaign contributions and to provide
appropriate identification on campaign mailings. It does not
otherwise regulate distribution of campaign material. Because
your question is not addressed by the Political Reform Act, we
cannot provide the opinion you have requested.

Sincerely,

Diane M. Griffiths
General Counsel

[signature]

By: Kathryn E. Donovan
Counsel, Legal Division

October 19, 1988


John Larson, Chairman
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 300
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

Dear Mr. Larson:

Please find enclosed information I received from my constituent,
Donald E. Ash, concerning his right as a political candidate to
distribute campaign material in mobilehome parks.

I would appreciate receiving an opinion from the Fair Political
Practices Commission concerning this point of law. Please
forward your response to me at my Riverside district office.

Thank you for your attention in this matter.

Sincerely,

ROBERT PRESLEY
State Senator

State of California
DEPARTMENT OF JUSTICE

P.O. BOX 944255
SACRAMENTO 94244-2550
Public Inquiry Unit (916) 322-3360

July 27, 1988

Toll Free—California Only:
800-952-5225


Donald E. Ash
1131 Mill St.
Lake Elsinore, CA  92330

Dear Mr. Ash:

Your letter of July 10, 1988 requests an opinion from the
Attorney General's Office. We regret that we will be unable to
provide the opinion you request.

Government Code section 12519 authorizes the Attorney General to
provide opinions only to designated public officers on questions
of law relating to their respective offices. It is for this
reason that we must respectfully decline your request.

Very truly yours,

JOHN K. VAN DE KAMP
Attorney General

[signed]
ROBERT M. RAYMER, Analyst
Public Inquiry Unit

RMR:ccc

1131 Hill St.
Lake Elsinore, Ca. 92330
July 10, 1986

Office of the Attorney General
1515 K St. Suite 511
Sacramento, Ca. 95814

Sir:

In a recent campaign for public office
I was not allowed to distribute my campaign
flyers to the residences in several mobile
home parks in this city. Despite my protests
that this was both undemocratic and probably
illegal, I was told by the managers that this
was private property, and, therefore, I was
restricted from entering the park for this
purpose.

Of course, every house in this community
is private property so that under that type
of pernicious reasoning, candidates for public
offices could be restricted from distributing
campaign material to any house. Such an idea
I think is foreign and not acceptable in a
free democratic society.

I feel my civil liberty to freely campaign
for a public office were infringed upon by
these parks and I would ask the opinion of
the Attorney General's office of this state
for an opinion on this issue.

Sincerely, [signature]
Donald J. Ash

State of California

Memorandum

To      :   Kathy Donovan                                    Date  :  October 25, 1988


From  :   Fair Political Practices Commission
          John H. Larson, Chairman [signed]

Subject :  Letter received from Senator Presley

          Attached hereto is a letter received from Senator Presley
          asking that we respond to him regarding a question on
          distribution of campaign materials in mobile home parks.

          I don't think this is within our jurisdiction and can be
          disposed of with a rather quick letter.

California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

November 8, 1988


Honorable Robert Presley
Senator, Thirty-sixth District
3600 Lime Street, Suite 111
Riverside, CA 92501

Re: Your Request for Assistance
Our File No. I-88-426

Dear Senator Presley:

We have received your letter requesting an opinion
concerning the rights of political candidates to distribute
campaign material in mobilehome parks. We are unable to assist
you in this matter because the subject is outside of the
Commission's jurisdiction.

The Commission is charged with the administration and
enforcement of the Political Reform Act. (Government Code
Sections 81000-91015.) The Political Reform Act requires
candidates to report campaign contributions and to provide
appropriate identification on campaign mailings. It does not
otherwise regulate distribution of campaign material. Because
your question is not addressed by the Political Reform Act, we
cannot provide the opinion you have requested.

Sincerely,

Diane M. Griffiths
General Counsel

[illegible]
By: Kathryn E. Donovan
Counsel, Legal Division

home parks in this city. Despite my protests

California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

November 8, 1988


Roland Bowns, Chief Counsel
Legal Office
California State Lottery
600 North 10th Street
Sacramento, CA 95814

                                                Re: 88-427

Dear Mr. Bowns:

     Your letter requesting advice under the Political Reform
Act was received on November 4, 1988 by the Fair Political
Practices Commission.  If you have any questions about your
advice request, you may contact me directly at (916) 322-5662.

     We try to answer all advice requests promptly.  Therefore,
unless your request poses particularly complex legal questions
or more information is needed, you should expect a response
within 21 working days if your request seeks formal written
advice. If more information is needed, the person assigned to
prepare a response to your request will contact you shortly to
advise you as to the information needed.  If your request is
for informal assistance, we will answer it as quickly as we
can.  (See Commission Regulation 18329 (2 Cal. Code of Regs.
Sec. 18329).)

     You also should be aware that your letter and our response
are public records which may be disclosed to the public upon
receipt of a proper request for disclosure.

                                                Very truly yours,

                                                [illegible]

                                                Jeanne Pritchard
                                                Chief
                                                Technical Assistance and Analysis
                                                Division

JP:plh


428 J Street, Suite 800 ● P.O. Box 807 ● Sacramento CA 95804-0807 ● (916) 322-5660

STATE OF CALIFORNIA                                    GEORGE DEUKMEJIAN, Governor

CALIFORNIA STATE LOTTERY
600 North 10th Street
Sacramento, CA 95814

                                                                 FPPC

                                                        Nov 4 2 51 PH '09


November 1, 1988


Diane Griffiths
Chief Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Ms. Griffiths:

     The California State Lottery has received an invitation from KFOY Radio
Station to send two Lottery representatives to a KFOY-sponsored event. This
offer includes air transportation provided by KFOY and an opportunity to meet
other KFOY clients at this event.

     Regarding the reporting requirements for designated employees who may be
subject to disclosure, and after reviewing the 1987/88 Manual for Statements of
Economic Interests for Designated Employees Form 730, we are of the
determination that if the California State Lottery accepts the invitation to attend
the event, and KFOY provides air transportation, the designated employees
would not be required to report these as gifts or income

     It is also our understanding that the employees chosen to represent the Lottery
at this event should not claim per diem for meals provided at no cost to the
employee.

     Before accepting this invitation, we would appreciate your legal opinion
regarding the disclosure requirements as outlined above.

Sincerely,


Roland Bowns
Chief Counsel
Legal Office

The sender expresses concern about a letter sent to the office of the Attorney General that received a cavalier response indicating a complete lack of consideration or concern to address a fundamental question in a democracy. As a candidate yourself, the sender hopes you can be of assistance in working to rectify this as it would seem to be an essential and crucial right of a candidate running for public office.
